sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage: A biochemist studies mitochondrial energy production. She traces electrons from NADH and FADH₂ through the electron transport chain (ETC) complexes I–IV on the inner mitochondrial membrane. Complex I (NADH dehydrogenase) accepts electrons from NADH, Complex II (succinate dehydrogenase) from FADH₂, and both pass electrons to ubiquinone (CoQ). Complex III (cytochrome bc₁) transfers electrons to cytochrome c, which delivers them to Complex IV (cytochrome c oxidase), where O₂ is the final electron acceptor, forming H₂O. Complexes I, III, and IV pump H⁺ from the matrix to the intermembrane space, generating the proton-motive force. ATP synthase (Complex V) uses this gradient to drive ATP synthesis by chemiosmosis. She measures a P/O ratio of ~2.5 for NADH and ~1.5 for FADH₂, reflecting the number of ATP produced per oxygen atom reduced.
questions.stem: The proton-motive force that drives ATP synthesis is generated by:

choices.label: A
choices.text: pumping of H⁺ ions from the mitochondrial matrix to the intermembrane space by Complexes I, III, and IV — creating an electrochemical gradient (ΔpH + Δψ) across the inner membrane

choices.label: B
choices.text: passive diffusion of H⁺ from the intermembrane space back into the matrix without any protein channel

choices.label: C
choices.text: pumping of H⁺ into the mitochondrial matrix, making the matrix more acidic

choices.label: D
choices.text: direct transfer of phosphate groups from the ETC complexes to ADP

explanations.A: Correct. Complexes I, III, and IV use the energy released from electron transfer to pump H⁺ from the matrix to the intermembrane space (IMS). This creates two components of the proton-motive force (Δp): a chemical gradient (ΔpH — higher [H⁺] in IMS, lower in matrix) and an electrical gradient (Δψ — positive charge in IMS, negative in matrix). Together, Δp ≈ -220 mV, providing the thermodynamic driving force for H⁺ to flow back through ATP synthase. Complex II does not pump protons, which is why FADH₂ yields fewer ATP than NADH.
explanations.B: H⁺ does flow back into the matrix, but only through ATP synthase (Complex V) — a specific protein channel that couples proton flow to ATP synthesis. Passive diffusion through the lipid bilayer would dissipate the gradient without producing ATP (uncoupling). Uncoupling proteins (UCPs) in brown adipose tissue do this deliberately to generate heat (thermogenin/UCP1).
explanations.C: Protons are pumped out of the matrix, making the matrix more alkaline (higher pH), not more acidic. The intermembrane space becomes more acidic. This asymmetry is critical — the matrix pH (~8) vs IMS pH (~7) contributes to the proton-motive force that drives ATP synthesis.
explanations.D: This describes substrate-level phosphorylation (direct transfer of a phosphate to ADP), not oxidative phosphorylation. The ETC complexes generate a proton gradient; ATP synthase then uses the gradient energy to phosphorylate ADP. No phosphate is transferred directly from ETC complexes to ADP.

questions.stem: NADH enters the ETC at Complex I, while FADH₂ enters at Complex II. FADH₂ produces fewer ATP (~1.5 vs ~2.5 per molecule) because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: FADH₂ has more energy than NADH and therefore requires fewer proton pumps

choices.label: B
choices.text: FADH₂ bypasses Complex I, so fewer protons are pumped — only Complexes III and IV contribute to the gradient for FADH₂-derived electrons, reducing the total proton-motive force per electron pair

choices.label: C
choices.text: FADH₂ donates electrons directly to O₂ without passing through any complexes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Complex II pumps twice as many protons as Complex I, compensating for the shorter chain

explanations.A: FADH₂ has less free energy than NADH (E°' = +0.03 V for FAD/FADH₂ vs -0.32 V for NAD⁺/NADH). Less energy means electrons enter the chain at a lower energy point (Complex II, not Complex I), and fewer proton pumps are driven.
explanations.B: Correct. FADH₂ donates electrons to Complex II (succinate dehydrogenase), which transfers them to ubiquinone (CoQ) without pumping any protons. From CoQ, electrons proceed through Complexes III and IV (both pump H⁺). Since Complex I is bypassed and Complex II does not pump, FADH₂-derived electrons drive fewer total proton translocations (~6 H⁺ vs ~10 H⁺ for NADH), producing ~1.5 ATP vs ~2.5 ATP. This is why the TCA cycle step that produces FADH₂ (succinate → fumarate via Complex II/SDH) contributes less to ATP than the NADH-generating steps.
explanations.C: FADH₂ electrons pass through Complex II → CoQ → Complex III → cytochrome c → Complex IV → O₂. They do not skip to O₂ directly. The chain of electron carriers is essential for coupling electron transfer to proton pumping.
explanations.D: Complex II does not pump any protons — it is the only ETC complex without proton-pumping activity. This is precisely why FADH₂ yields fewer ATP. Complex I pumps ~4 H⁺ per NADH, which FADH₂ electrons miss entirely.

questions.stem: Cyanide poisoning is lethal because cyanide:

choices.label: A
choices.text: blocks Complex II, preventing succinate oxidation

choices.label: B
choices.text: uncouples the proton gradient from ATP synthesis like DNP

choices.label: C
choices.text: irreversibly inhibits Complex IV (cytochrome c oxidase), preventing electrons from reaching O₂ — the entire ETC backs up because no terminal electron acceptor is available, halting oxidative phosphorylation

choices.label: D
choices.text: destroys the inner mitochondrial membrane, releasing all matrix enzymes

explanations.A: Complex II inhibitors include malonate (a competitive inhibitor of succinate dehydrogenase). Cyanide does not affect Complex II. Even if Complex II were blocked, electrons from NADH through Complex I would still flow.
explanations.B: Uncouplers (DNP, FCCP, thermogenin/UCP1) dissipate the proton gradient by allowing H⁺ to leak across the inner membrane without passing through ATP synthase. Cyanide is an ETC inhibitor, not an uncoupler — it blocks electron flow, whereas uncouplers allow electron flow but disconnect it from ATP synthesis.
explanations.C: Correct. Cyanide (CN⁻) binds the iron in cytochrome a₃ of Complex IV, blocking electron transfer to O₂. Without a functional terminal electron acceptor, all upstream carriers become fully reduced (electron traffic jam), the ETC stops, and no proton gradient is maintained. ATP production by oxidative phosphorylation ceases. Cells switch to anaerobic glycolysis (only 2 ATP/glucose), which is insufficient for tissues with high energy demands (brain, heart). Other Complex IV inhibitors include carbon monoxide (CO) and hydrogen sulfide (H₂S). Treatment: hydroxocobalamin or nitrites (which generate methemoglobin to scavenge CN⁻).
explanations.D: Cyanide does not disrupt membrane integrity. It specifically binds the heme a₃ center of Complex IV. The inner membrane remains intact; the problem is that electron transfer through Complex IV is blocked.

questions.stem: ATP synthase (Complex V) produces ATP by:

choices.label: A
choices.text: directly oxidizing NADH in its active site

choices.label: B
choices.text: transferring electrons from cytochrome c to O₂

choices.label: C
choices.text: hydrolyzing GTP to GDP, then transferring the phosphate to ADP

choices.label: D
choices.text: harnessing the flow of H⁺ down their electrochemical gradient through the F₀ channel, which drives rotation of the F₁ catalytic head — this mechanical rotation causes conformational changes that bind ADP + Pi and release ATP

explanations.A: NADH is oxidized at Complex I (NADH dehydrogenase), not at ATP synthase. ATP synthase does not directly interact with NADH or any electron carrier — it uses the proton gradient, not electrons.
explanations.B: Electron transfer from cytochrome c to O₂ is the function of Complex IV (cytochrome c oxidase). ATP synthase is Complex V and handles only proton translocation and ATP synthesis.
explanations.C: GTP hydrolysis to power ATP synthesis does not occur at ATP synthase. GTP is produced by substrate-level phosphorylation in the TCA cycle (succinyl-CoA synthetase), and nucleoside diphosphate kinase can convert GTP to ATP, but this is independent of ATP synthase.
explanations.D: Correct. ATP synthase is a molecular rotary motor with two functional domains: F₀ (membrane-embedded proton channel, containing the c-ring) and F₁ (matrix-facing catalytic head, containing the αβ subunits). Proton flow through F₀ drives rotation of the c-ring and γ-subunit, which causes sequential conformational changes in the three β-subunits of F₁: open (empty) → loose (binds ADP + Pi) → tight (catalyzes ATP formation) → open (releases ATP). This binding-change mechanism (Boyer) produces ~3 ATP per full 360° rotation (~10 H⁺). ATP synthase was confirmed as a rotary motor by Yoshida's fluorescent actin filament experiment.

questions.stem: The total ATP yield from complete oxidation of one glucose molecule is approximately:

choices.label: A
choices.text: 2 ATP (from glycolysis only)

choices.label: B
choices.text: 4 ATP (from substrate-level phosphorylation in glycolysis and the TCA cycle)

choices.label: C
choices.text: 30-32 ATP — combining substrate-level phosphorylation (glycolysis: 2 ATP, TCA: 2 GTP) with oxidative phosphorylation from 10 NADH (~25 ATP) and 2 FADH₂ (~3 ATP)

choices.label: D
choices.text: 100 ATP per glucose

explanations.A: 2 ATP is the net yield from glycolysis alone (anaerobic conditions). Complete aerobic oxidation produces ~15-16 times more ATP by harnessing the electron carriers through the ETC.
explanations.B: 4 ATP counts only substrate-level phosphorylation (2 from glycolysis + 2 GTP from TCA) and ignores oxidative phosphorylation, which accounts for the vast majority (~85-90%) of ATP from glucose.
explanations.C: Correct. Complete glucose oxidation: glycolysis (2 ATP + 2 NADH), pyruvate decarboxylation (2 NADH), TCA cycle ×2 (2 GTP + 6 NADH + 2 FADH₂). Total electron carriers: 10 NADH × 2.5 ATP = 25 ATP; 2 FADH₂ × 1.5 ATP = 3 ATP. Substrate-level: 2 ATP + 2 GTP = 4 ATP. Grand total: 25 + 3 + 4 = 32 ATP (maximum). The actual yield is ~30-32 because the glycolytic NADH shuttle (malate-aspartate vs glycerol-3-phosphate) affects whether cytoplasmic NADH yields 2.5 or 1.5 ATP. The malate-aspartate shuttle (liver, heart) preserves the full 2.5; the glycerol-3-phosphate shuttle (muscle, brain) yields only 1.5.
explanations.D: 100 ATP per glucose is far above the thermodynamic maximum. The free energy of glucose oxidation (ΔG°' = -2870 kJ/mol) and the energy stored per ATP (~30.5 kJ/mol) set a theoretical maximum around 94 ATP, but actual efficiency is ~30-32 due to energy losses at each step.

questions.stem: Uncoupling proteins (UCPs) in brown adipose tissue generate heat by:

choices.label: A
choices.text: increasing the activity of ATP synthase to produce excess ATP, which is then hydrolyzed for heat

choices.label: B
choices.text: blocking Complex IV so electrons cannot reach oxygen

choices.label: C
choices.text: providing an alternative pathway for H⁺ to flow back into the matrix without passing through ATP synthase — dissipating the proton gradient as heat instead of ATP

choices.label: D
choices.text: inhibiting the TCA cycle so acetyl-CoA accumulates

explanations.A: Uncoupling reduces ATP synthesis, it does not increase it. The proton gradient energy is wasted as heat instead of driving ATP synthase. UCPs and ATP synthase are alternative pathways for proton return — they compete for the same gradient.
explanations.B: Blocking Complex IV describes an ETC inhibitor (like cyanide), not an uncoupler. UCPs do not affect electron transport — the ETC runs normally. The uncoupling occurs at the level of the proton gradient, not electron flow.
explanations.C: Correct. UCP1 (thermogenin) in brown adipose tissue creates a proton leak across the inner mitochondrial membrane. H⁺ flows down the gradient through UCP1 instead of ATP synthase. The energy stored in the gradient is released as thermal energy (heat) rather than being captured in ATP. This is non-shivering thermogenesis, essential for neonates and hibernating mammals. The ETC continues operating (O₂ is still consumed, NADH is still oxidized), but the energy is uncoupled from ATP production. Chemical uncouplers like DNP work by the same principle — they are lipophilic weak acids that shuttle H⁺ across the membrane.
explanations.D: UCPs do not inhibit the TCA cycle. In fact, because ATP synthesis is reduced, the cell compensates by increasing TCA cycle flux and fatty acid oxidation to maintain the proton gradient that is being dissipated. Metabolic rate increases, not decreases.

choices.label: A
choices.text: ATP is produced by direct chemical coupling between ETC complexes and ADP

choices.label: B
choices.text: ATP synthesis is driven by a proton electrochemical gradient across the inner mitochondrial membrane — the ETC generates the gradient, and ATP synthase uses it to phosphorylate ADP

choices.label: C
choices.text: ATP is produced only in the cytoplasm, not in mitochondria

choices.label: D
choices.text: electrons flow from O₂ to NADH, releasing energy for ATP synthesis

explanations.A: Direct chemical coupling was the prevailing hypothesis before Mitchell. It predicted a high-energy chemical intermediate linking the ETC to ATP synthesis. No such intermediate was ever found. Mitchell showed that the proton gradient itself is the 'intermediate' — an indirect, electrochemical coupling.
explanations.B: Correct. Peter Mitchell's chemiosmotic hypothesis (1961, Nobel Prize 1978) proposed that the energy from electron transport is not directly coupled to ATP synthesis through chemical intermediates (as previously believed) but is stored as a transmembrane electrochemical proton gradient (proton-motive force). This was revolutionary because it introduced an indirect coupling mechanism: (1) ETC complexes pump H⁺ across the membrane; (2) the resulting Δp drives H⁺ back through ATP synthase; (3) this proton flow powers the mechanical rotation that synthesizes ATP. Evidence: uncouplers dissipate the gradient without inhibiting the ETC, and artificially imposed pH gradients can drive ATP synthesis without electron transport.
explanations.C: The majority of ATP is produced in mitochondria (oxidative phosphorylation on the inner membrane). Only substrate-level phosphorylation in glycolysis occurs in the cytoplasm (2 ATP/glucose), accounting for a small fraction of total ATP.
explanations.D: Electrons flow from NADH to O₂, not the reverse. NADH (strong reducing agent, E°' = -0.32 V) donates electrons, and O₂ (strong oxidizing agent, E°' = +0.82 V) accepts them. The overall ΔE°' = +1.14 V is highly favorable, releasing ~220 kJ/mol of energy to pump protons.

questions.stem: The biochemist measures oxygen consumption in isolated mitochondria. Adding NADH increases O₂ consumption. Subsequently adding oligomycin (an ATP synthase inhibitor) would:

choices.label: A
choices.text: increase O₂ consumption further because the ETC speeds up to compensate

choices.label: B
choices.text: have no effect on O₂ consumption

choices.label: C
choices.text: stop O₂ consumption entirely because it blocks Complex IV

choices.label: D
choices.text: decrease O₂ consumption — without ATP synthase activity, the proton gradient builds up and opposes further proton pumping by the ETC, slowing electron flow and O₂ reduction

explanations.A: Oligomycin slows, not speeds, the ETC. The ETC cannot pump protons against an unrelieved gradient. The system reaches a steady state where the gradient is too high for further pumping.
explanations.B: Oligomycin significantly affects O₂ consumption because the ETC and ATP synthase are coupled through the proton gradient. Blocking one component affects the other through gradient accumulation.
explanations.C: Oligomycin does not block Complex IV or any ETC complex. It specifically inhibits ATP synthase (Complex V). O₂ consumption decreases but does not stop entirely because some basal proton leak across the membrane partially relieves the gradient, allowing slow electron flow.
explanations.D: Correct. Oligomycin blocks the F₀ proton channel of ATP synthase, preventing H⁺ flow back into the matrix. Without this proton return pathway, the gradient builds to a maximum. The high Δp opposes further proton pumping by Complexes I, III, and IV (thermodynamically unfavorable to pump against an increasingly steep gradient). This backpressure slows electron transport, reducing O₂ consumption. Adding an uncoupler (DNP) after oligomycin would collapse the gradient and restore maximal O₂ consumption — a classic experiment distinguishing inhibitors from uncouplers.

questions.stem: Oxygen serves as the final electron acceptor in the ETC. Without oxygen:

choices.label: A
choices.text: all electron carriers become fully reduced and the ETC stops — NADH and FADH₂ accumulate, the TCA cycle and glycolysis slow due to NAD⁺ depletion, and cells rely on anaerobic glycolysis for ATP

choices.label: B
choices.text: the ETC continues to function normally using an alternative electron acceptor

choices.label: C
choices.text: ATP synthase increases its activity to compensate for the loss of electron flow

choices.label: D
choices.text: Complex I begins to accept electrons from O₂ in reverse

explanations.A: Correct. O₂ is the terminal electron acceptor at Complex IV. Without it, electrons cannot be removed from the chain, so all carriers (CoQ, cytochrome c, heme groups) become saturated/reduced. The ETC stops, no proton gradient is generated, and oxidative phosphorylation ceases. NAD⁺ is not regenerated from NADH, so the TCA cycle stalls (NAD⁺ is a required substrate for isocitrate dehydrogenase, α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase, and malate dehydrogenase). Cells switch to anaerobic glycolysis (glucose → 2 lactate + 2 ATP), which regenerates NAD⁺ via LDH but is far less efficient (~15× less ATP per glucose).
explanations.B: In human cells, there is no significant alternative to O₂ as a terminal electron acceptor. Some bacteria can use nitrate, sulfate, or fumarate as alternative acceptors (anaerobic respiration), but mammalian mitochondria require O₂.
explanations.C: ATP synthase requires the proton gradient to function. Without the ETC running, no gradient is maintained, and ATP synthase has no driving force. It cannot compensate for ETC failure.
explanations.D: Electron flow in the ETC is thermodynamically downhill: NADH → O₂ (ΔE°' = +1.14 V). Reverse flow (O₂ → Complex I) would require massive energy input and does not occur under physiological conditions. Reverse electron transfer can occur in specific experimental conditions but is not a physiological response to hypoxia.

questions.stem: The inner mitochondrial membrane is highly impermeable to most ions and polar molecules. This impermeability is essential because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: it allows the matrix to accumulate Ca²⁺ for muscle contraction

choices.label: B
choices.text: it prevents the proton gradient from dissipating — if H⁺ could freely cross the membrane, the proton-motive force would collapse and ATP synthase would have no driving force

choices.label: C
choices.text: it blocks all substrate entry into the mitochondria, ensuring only fatty acids are metabolized

choices.label: D
choices.text: it prevents oxygen from entering the matrix

explanations.A: While mitochondria do sequester Ca²⁺ (contributing to Ca²⁺ signaling and buffering), this is not why the inner membrane is impermeable. The primary functional significance is maintaining the proton gradient for ATP synthesis.
explanations.B: Correct. The impermeability of the inner mitochondrial membrane to H⁺ is the foundation of chemiosmotic coupling. If H⁺ leaked freely, the gradient generated by the ETC would dissipate as heat rather than driving ATP synthesis (this is exactly what uncoupling proteins do in a controlled manner). The tight lipid bilayer (enriched in cardiolipin, a unique phospholipid) maintains the seal. Specific transport proteins (adenine nucleotide translocase for ATP/ADP exchange, phosphate carrier, pyruvate carrier, malate-aspartate shuttle components) allow selective passage of required metabolites while maintaining proton impermeability.
explanations.C: The inner membrane has specific transporters for pyruvate (MPC), fatty acyl-carnitine (carnitine shuttle/CPT I and II), and other metabolites. It is not impermeable to all substrates — it is selectively permeable, admitting necessary substrates while blocking H⁺ and most ions.
explanations.D: O₂ is a small, nonpolar gas that freely diffuses across all biological membranes, including the inner mitochondrial membrane. No transporter is needed for O₂ entry. The membrane's impermeability applies to ions and polar/charged molecules, not to gases.
